title: Salesforce Is Getting Treated Like a Dinosaur While Agentforce Prints a Billion Dollars
subtitle: Down roughly 40% from its highs, CRM is trading at 11x forward earnings with $14B in free cash flow and the fastest-growing AI agent product in the enterprise — the market might have this one completely wrong.
summary: Salesforce is getting hammered by the 'SaaSpocalypse' narrative while Agentforce hits $1B ARR in under 12 months — the fastest product ramp in company history. With $14.4B in FCF, a $50B buyback, and an 11x forward P/E (half of MSFT and NOW), this is the most misunderstood AI play in the market.

image.caption: Salesforce Tower — the AI agent platform everyone's doubting just hit $1B ARR in under a year.

links.label: Morgan Stanley Cuts CRM PT 35% — Bloomberg

bodyHtml:
You ever watch the market lose its mind over something it absolutely does not understand?

That's Salesforce right now. The company that practically invented cloud CRM is getting treated like a dying legacy software vendor — trading at a valuation that makes zero sense for a business printing fourteen billion dollars in free cash flow with the fastest-growing AI product in enterprise history.

Let's back up. The "SaaSpocalypse" narrative has been making the rounds, and CRM got caught in the crossfire. Analysts are suddenly skeptical. Morgan Stanley slashed their price target by over a third. KeyBanc came out questioning whether Agentforce — Salesforce's AI agent platform — actually has the traction people think it does. The doubt is everywhere. The stock's been cut roughly 40% from where it was sitting at the highs. The narrative on the Street is clear: SaaS is dying, AI is overhyped, and Salesforce is yesterday's news.

Except the numbers tell a completely different story.

Agentforce hit a billion dollars in annualized revenue run rate in less than twelve months. Let me say that again — a billion dollars. In under a year. That's the fastest product ramp in Salesforce's entire history, and we're talking about a company that's been around since 1999. Over nine thousand five hundred paid Agentforce deals. Nearly twenty-nine trillion tokens processed per quarter — and that number is growing 152% quarter over quarter. If you're looking for evidence that enterprises are actually buying AI agents and not just talking about them, that's it. That's your proof. The stock even surged 5% in a single session earlier this month when the market caught a whiff of this acceleration. The data is there. People just aren't paying attention.

Meanwhile, the market is pricing this thing like it's headed for the scrap heap. Eleven times forward earnings. For a business growing 13% with seventy-eight percent gross margins and thirty-five percent free cash flow margins. Compare that to Microsoft and ServiceNow — both of which trade at roughly nineteen to twenty times earnings. That's nearly double the multiple for companies playing in the exact same AI agent space. The market is literally telling you that Salesforce's AI business is worth half of what its competitors' AI businesses are worth. That doesn't pass the smell test.

And it gets better. Or worse, depending on your perspective. Salesforce has a fifty-billion-dollar buyback authorization sitting on the table. They've already deployed over twelve and a half billion of it this fiscal year alone. That's not a company in trouble — that's a management team staring at their own valuation and deciding to eat their own cooking because they know it's absurdly cheap. When a company is buying back its own shares at this rate while simultaneously growing revenue at 13% and pumping out cash flow, you have to ask yourself: who's right here — the panicking algos or the people inside the building?

The analyst consensus is still a Buy with a mean target suggesting roughly forty-five percent upside from where shares trade today. That's not one or two optimists — that's the Street average. The same analysts who've been talking down enterprise SaaS are, on aggregate, saying CRM is a screaming buy at these levels. The bull case isn't fringe. It's the baseline.

Now let's talk competition. Microsoft has Copilot. ServiceNow has its own AI agent platform. Both are solid products with serious enterprise traction. But here's what nobody's connecting: both MSFT and NOW trade at roughly double CRM's forward P/E despite generating similar or worse growth rates in their AI agent businesses. The market has decided Salesforce is the ugly stepchild of enterprise AI. The data says otherwise. If you believe AI agents are the next big thing in enterprise software — and most people do — then owning the cheapest pure-play on that thesis is a bet that eventually, the math wins.

At the end of the day, this is a story about perception versus reality. The perception is that Salesforce is a legacy CRM vendor getting squeezed by the AI revolution. The reality is a company generating over forty-one billion dollars in revenue with a fourteen-billion-dollar free cash flow engine, seventy-eight percent gross margins, the fastest-growing AI agent platform on the market, and a valuation that assumes zero future growth. That's not an investment thesis. That's a mismatch. And mismatches like this don't last forever.

Disclosure: The Signal holds no position in CRM. Positions may change. This is not financial advice.
